texts.orig: SEPARATION OF  ORGANIC VAPORS FROM  AIR*
texts.text: SEPARATION OF  ORGANIC VAPORS FROM  AIR*

texts.text: (Received  July 21, 1986; accepted in revised form October 21,1986)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the early 197Os,  the industries producing  solvent-containing air exhaust streams have  been  under  increasing  economic and  regulatory  pressure.  One problem  is the increasing  cost  of  energy.  Many  of  these  streams are produced in high-temperature  industrial  ovens,  where, because  of the explosion  danger, strict limits  govern  the  concentration of  solvent  vapors  permitted. The usual method  used to stay within  these limits is to ventilate  the oven  chamber  with fresh  air in  sufficient  volume  to  dilute the  vapor  concentration to  acceptable safe levels.  This  method  wastes large amounts  of process  heat  in the exhaust gas.  Of  even  greater  economic significance is the  solvent  contained in  these exhaust  streams. In the past,  when  air pollution  regulations  were lax and the solvents were  inexpensive, these  organic solvent  vapors were  simply  discharged.  Presently,  however,  some  form  of  treatment  is required  to  meet  air pollution  standards.  Most  existing  treatment  processes  only  prevent  air pollution,  and  despite  the  increased  value  of  the  solvent,  its recovery  is still not economically  practical  except  for very large exhaust  streams and under particularly favorable  conditions.
texts.text: Since the early 197Os,  the industries producing  solvent-containing air exhaust streams have  been  under  increasing  economic and  regulatory  pressure.  One problem  is the increasing  cost  of  energy.  Many  of  these  streams are produced in high-temperature  industrial  ovens,  where, because  of the explosion  danger, strict limits  govern  the  concentration of  solvent  vapors  permitted. The usual method  used to stay within  these limits is to ventilate  the oven  chamber  with fresh  air in  sufficient  volume  to  dilute the  vapor  concentration to  acceptable safe levels.  This  method  wastes large amounts  of process  heat  in the exhaust gas.  Of  even  greater  economic significance is the  solvent  contained in  these exhaust  streams. In the past,  when  air pollution  regulations  were lax and the solvents were  inexpensive, these  organic solvent  vapors were  simply  discharged.  Presently,  however,  some  form  of  treatment  is required  to  meet  air pollution  standards.  Most  existing  treatment  processes  only  prevent  air pollution,  and  despite  the  increased  value  of  the  solvent,  its recovery  is still not economically  practical  except  for very large exhaust  streams and under particularly favorable  conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  volume  of  solvents  involved  in these processes  is very  large and  represents a significant  reuse opportunity.  Total  nationwide  emissions for 1975 were estimated  by  the  Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to  be  about  31 million  tons [ 11.  Based  on  the  EPA's  reports,  it is clear  that  the  sources  of organic  solvent  emissions  are very  fragmented.  Petroleum  refining  is the largest single  emission  source,  producing  approximately  ten percent  of the treatable total.  However,  no  other  single  industry  or  process  produces  more  than one to two percent  of the total  nationwide  emissions.
texts.text: The  volume  of  solvents  involved  in these processes  is very  large and  represents a significant  reuse opportunity.  Total  nationwide  emissions for 1975 were estimated  by  the  Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to  be  about  31 million  tons [ 11.  Based  on  the  EPA's  reports,  it is clear  that  the  sources  of organic  solvent  emissions  are very  fragmented.  Petroleum  refining  is the largest single  emission  source,  producing  approximately  ten percent  of the treatable total.  However,  no  other  single  industry  or  process  produces  more  than one to two percent  of the total  nationwide  emissions.

texts.orig: Although  the  sources  of  solvent  emissions  are  varied,  the  composition of emission  streams  is homogeneous [ 21. Among  the  large number  of  solvents used  industrially,  naphtha  represents  more  than  half  of  the  total  emissions,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and the eight solvents  naphtha,  toluene,  xylene,  perchlorethylene, trichloroethane,  ethyl  alcohol,  methyl  alcohol,  and  acetone  together  represent  almost eighty  percent  of  the  total  solvent  emissions.  We  conclude  that  a membrane process  capable  of treating  naphtha  and one  or two others  from  this short  list of  solvents  would  have a substantial  economic  impact.
texts.text: and the eight solvents  naphtha,  toluene,  xylene,  perchlorethylene, trichloroethane,  ethyl  alcohol,  methyl  alcohol,  and  acetone  together  represent  almost eighty  percent  of  the  total  solvent  emissions.  We  conclude  that  a membrane process  capable  of treating  naphtha  and one  or two others  from  this short  list of  solvents  would  have a substantial  economic  impact.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The process  we are developing  is shown schematically  in Fig. 1. Hot  solventladen  exhaust  streams from  solvent-drying ovens  are passed  over the  surface of a membrane much more permeable to organic vapors than to air. The organic vapors  pass through  the membrane  and are condensed  to recover  the solvent. The  air and remaining  solvent  are recycled  to the oven.
texts.text: The process  we are developing  is shown schematically  in Fig. 1. Hot  solventladen  exhaust  streams from  solvent-drying ovens  are passed  over the  surface of a membrane much more permeable to organic vapors than to air. The organic vapors  pass through  the membrane  and are condensed  to recover  the solvent. The  air and remaining  solvent  are recycled  to the oven.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the process  shown  in Fig. 1 is to be sucessful,  membranes  are required that are  much  more  permeable  to  organic  vapors  than  to  air.  In  this  paper,  the results leading to the selection  of  these  membrane  materials  are described.  In a subsequent  paper,  we will describe  the preparation  of  thin  composite  membranes,  small membrane  modules,  and  the bench-scale demonstration of  the process.
texts.text: If the process  shown  in Fig. 1 is to be sucessful,  membranes  are required that are  much  more  permeable  to  organic  vapors  than  to  air.  In  this  paper,  the results leading to the selection  of  these  membrane  materials  are described.  In a subsequent  paper,  we will describe  the preparation  of  thin  composite  membranes,  small membrane  modules,  and  the bench-scale demonstration of  the process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted  that in the work  reported  here, we have calculated  membrane  selectivities  from  the ratio of  nitrogen  and organic  vapor  permeabilities obtained  separately.  It might be expected  that the permeability  of nitrogen  in the presence  of an organic vapor would be quite different  from the permeability obtained  with pure nitrogen.  For the purpose  of this material selection  process we decided  to ignore  this effect.  Fortunately,  in subsequent  experiments  with membrane  modules  and mixed  nitrogen-organic vapor  feed  streams, we have found  the effect  of  organic  vapors  on  the  nitrogen  and  oxygen  permeabilities of  these membranes  to be surprisingly  small.
texts.text: It should be noted  that in the work  reported  here, we have calculated  membrane  selectivities  from  the ratio of  nitrogen  and organic  vapor  permeabilities obtained  separately.  It might be expected  that the permeability  of nitrogen  in the presence  of an organic vapor would be quite different  from the permeability obtained  with pure nitrogen.  For the purpose  of this material selection  process we decided  to ignore  this effect.  Fortunately,  in subsequent  experiments  with membrane  modules  and mixed  nitrogen-organic vapor  feed  streams, we have found  the effect  of  organic  vapors  on  the  nitrogen  and  oxygen  permeabilities of  these membranes  to be surprisingly  small.

texts.text: Fig. 1. A solvent and waste heat recovery system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  membrane  test  apparatus  shown  schematically in  Fig.  2 was  used  to measure nitrogen  and solvent permeabilities.  The permeability  apparatus consists of  a sealed  glass system  maintained  at low pressure  by  a vacuum  pump. The  system  was  designed  to  measure  the  gas permeability  by  measuring  the rate of  loss from  the  high-pressure (feed) side of  the  membrane.  To  simplify the experimental  procedure,  gas and vapor permeabilities  were determined  for the  gas  (nitrogen) and  the  solvents  separately  using  pure  single-component gas or vapor  feeds.
texts.text: The  membrane  test  apparatus  shown  schematically in  Fig.  2 was  used  to measure nitrogen  and solvent permeabilities.  The permeability  apparatus consists of  a sealed  glass system  maintained  at low pressure  by  a vacuum  pump. The  system  was  designed  to  measure  the  gas permeability  by  measuring  the rate of  loss from  the  high-pressure (feed) side of  the  membrane.  To  simplify the experimental  procedure,  gas and vapor permeabilities  were determined  for the  gas  (nitrogen) and  the  solvents  separately  using  pure  single-component gas or vapor  feeds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In both the gas and vapor permeability  measurements,  the feed-side pressure was first brought  to an appropriate  level by letting gas or vapor  enter from the gas line. The permeate  side of the system was continuously  evacuated  with the vacuum pump.  As gas or vapor  permeated  the membrane,  the pressure  on the feed  side  of  the  membrane  fell.  The  mercury  level  in  the  Toepler  pump  was then raised to restore the feed pressure to its original  value. The  change  in the mercury  level  over  time  could  thus  be  used  to  calculate  the  gas or vapor  flux through  the membrane.
texts.text: In both the gas and vapor permeability  measurements,  the feed-side pressure was first brought  to an appropriate  level by letting gas or vapor  enter from the gas line. The permeate  side of the system was continuously  evacuated  with the vacuum pump.  As gas or vapor  permeated  the membrane,  the pressure  on the feed  side  of  the  membrane  fell.  The  mercury  level  in  the  Toepler  pump  was then raised to restore the feed pressure to its original  value. The  change  in the mercury  level  over  time  could  thus  be  used  to  calculate  the  gas or vapor  flux through  the membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All  of  the  candidate  membranes  were screened  by  measuring  their permeabilities to nitrogen, toluene, octane,  acetone, and trichloroethane  at 40' C. These solvents  were  chosen  because  they  represent  more  than  80%  of  all  solvents now used. The  nitrogen,  trichloroethane, and toluene permeabilities  of the two best polymers  were also measured  at temperatures  up to  155  a  C.
texts.text: All  of  the  candidate  membranes  were screened  by  measuring  their permeabilities to nitrogen, toluene, octane,  acetone, and trichloroethane  at 40' C. These solvents  were  chosen  because  they  represent  more  than  80%  of  all  solvents now used. The  nitrogen,  trichloroethane, and toluene permeabilities  of the two best polymers  were also measured  at temperatures  up to  155  a  C.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Gas and  solvent  permeabilities of  the  membranes  tested  ranged  over  six orders  of  magnitude.  With  care,  however,  we could  normally  obtain  permeabilities  reproducible  to  within  20%,  and  this  was  sufficient  for  our  screening studies.
texts.text: Gas and  solvent  permeabilities of  the  membranes  tested  ranged  over  six orders  of  magnitude.  With  care,  however,  we could  normally  obtain  permeabilities  reproducible  to  within  20%,  and  this  was  sufficient  for  our  screening studies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymer  membranes  useful  for  the  separation  of  organic  vapors  from  air should be highly permeable  to organic  solvents,  but relatively  impermeable  to air. Secondly,  because  many  of the organic  vapor  streams we propose  to treat are hot,  the polymer  should  be able to withstand  high temperatures  ideally up to 200 o  C. Finally,  in order to prepare  thin  membranes  by the solvent-casting  technique we  propose to  use,  the  polymer should  be  soluble  in  organic solvents.
texts.text: Polymer  membranes  useful  for  the  separation  of  organic  vapors  from  air should be highly permeable  to organic  solvents,  but relatively  impermeable  to air. Secondly,  because  many  of the organic  vapor  streams we propose  to treat are hot,  the polymer  should  be able to withstand  high temperatures  ideally up to 200 o  C. Finally,  in order to prepare  thin  membranes  by the solvent-casting  technique we  propose to  use,  the  polymer should  be  soluble  in  organic solvents.

texts.orig: There  is a great amount  of  data in the literature  concerning  permeation  of gases through  polymer  membranes,  including  several  recent  reviews [ 3-51. There  are much  fewer data on the permeation  of  organic  vapors,  and the factors  that  affect  vapor  permeation are  less well  understood. Considering  the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: factors  affecting the  components D and K  of the  permeability is a useful  place to  begin rationalizing vapor  permeation. The diffusion coefficient tends to decrease  with increasing  permeant  diameter,  since  large molecules  interact  with more  segments of  the  polymer chain and  are  thus less  mobile. On  the  other hand,  the  partition coefficient of gases  increases with the  condensability of the gas. With  simple  gases  these  two effects  are  comparable, but  with  vapors  sorption  effects  seem  to dominate.  This  is shown  by the  data  in Fig.  3, which  shows a plot  of gas  and  vapor  permeability vs.  vapor  critical point  temperature. The vapor  critical  point  temperature is a  measure  of the  condensability and  hence sorption of the  vapor.  These two  sets  of  data [ 6,  71  represent practically the only  extensive data  on vapor  and  gas permeabilities available  in the  literature, and both  clearly  show a dramatic  increase  in permeability with increasedvapor critical  point.  The  data  also  shows that  vapor permeabilities can  be be expected to be 100  to  10,000  times  higher  than  nitrogen  or oxygen  permeabilities for this type  of rubbery  polymer.
texts.text: factors  affecting the  components D and K  of the  permeability is a useful  place to  begin rationalizing vapor  permeation. The diffusion coefficient tends to decrease  with increasing  permeant  diameter,  since  large molecules  interact  with more  segments of  the  polymer chain and  are  thus less  mobile. On  the  other hand,  the  partition coefficient of gases  increases with the  condensability of the gas. With  simple  gases  these  two effects  are  comparable, but  with  vapors  sorption  effects  seem  to dominate.  This  is shown  by the  data  in Fig.  3, which  shows a plot  of gas  and  vapor  permeability vs.  vapor  critical point  temperature. The vapor  critical  point  temperature is a  measure  of the  condensability and  hence sorption of the  vapor.  These two  sets  of  data [ 6,  71  represent practically the only  extensive data  on vapor  and  gas permeabilities available  in the  literature, and both  clearly  show a dramatic  increase  in permeability with increasedvapor critical  point.  The  data  also  shows that  vapor permeabilities can  be be expected to be 100  to  10,000  times  higher  than  nitrogen  or oxygen  permeabilities for this type  of rubbery  polymer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based  on these  data,  we chose  to screen  a series  of high-temperature rubbers for  their  ability  to  separate organic  vapors  from  air.  The  materials chosen  are listed  in  Table 1.  Membranes used  in  the  screening studies  were  prepared  by casting  25 to  50 pm thick  films  onto  glass  plates  using  a Gardner  casting  knife.
texts.text: Based  on these  data,  we chose  to screen  a series  of high-temperature rubbers for  their  ability  to  separate organic  vapors  from  air.  The  materials chosen  are listed  in  Table 1.  Membranes used  in  the  screening studies  were  prepared  by casting  25 to  50 pm thick  films  onto  glass  plates  using  a Gardner  casting  knife.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: solvents,  Among  the most important  criteria is membrane  selectivity.  A selectivity of greater than  100 to 200 is required if good  separation  of organic  vapors from  the  air stream is to be  obtained.  However,  selectivities  greater than  500 to 1,000 are not required, since very little further increase in the concentration of  organic  vapor  in  the permeate  will  be  obtained.  Of  course,  we also  require the highest possible  solvent  permeability  commensurate  with adequate  selectivity. Finally, the ease with which ultra-thin  membranes  can be prepared from the  material  by  casting  or  some  other  technique is  also  important. Silicone rubber,  for  example,  can  be  easily  fabricated into  films  0.5  to  2.0 pm  thick, while a sticky,  rubbery  material such as Hypalon@  is very difficult  to fabricate into  defect-free films.
texts.text: solvents,  Among  the most important  criteria is membrane  selectivity.  A selectivity of greater than  100 to 200 is required if good  separation  of organic  vapors from  the  air stream is to be  obtained.  However,  selectivities  greater than  500 to 1,000 are not required, since very little further increase in the concentration of  organic  vapor  in  the permeate  will  be  obtained.  Of  course,  we also  require the highest possible  solvent  permeability  commensurate  with adequate  selectivity. Finally, the ease with which ultra-thin  membranes  can be prepared from the  material  by  casting  or  some  other  technique is  also  important. Silicone rubber,  for  example,  can  be  easily  fabricated into  films  0.5  to  2.0 pm  thick, while a sticky,  rubbery  material such as Hypalon@  is very difficult  to fabricate into  defect-free films.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For  solvents  such  as acetone  and  trichloroethane, only  Fluorel@,  Hypalon, and neoprene  have  adequate  selectivities.  Hypalon  is unsuitable  because  it is difficult  to make  into  thin  films;  the best material  is Fluorel,  followed  by  neoprene.  For  solvents  such  as  toluene  and  octane,  a  number  of  the  materials tested have adequate selectivities.  Of these materials, those with the best fluxes are silicone  rubber  and  neoprene.  Because  of  the  ease  with  which  ultra-thin silicone  rubber membranes  can be made, this material would be the first choice.
texts.text: For  solvents  such  as acetone  and  trichloroethane, only  Fluorel@,  Hypalon, and neoprene  have  adequate  selectivities.  Hypalon  is unsuitable  because  it is difficult  to make  into  thin  films;  the best material  is Fluorel,  followed  by  neoprene.  For  solvents  such  as  toluene  and  octane,  a  number  of  the  materials tested have adequate selectivities.  Of these materials, those with the best fluxes are silicone  rubber  and  neoprene.  Because  of  the  ease  with  which  ultra-thin silicone  rubber membranes  can be made, this material would be the first choice.
